All right, you're back for the next lesson. We're in a great spot here. We left off with this idea of how many loops should you run, right? And my answer was, well, theoretically, the number is actually infinite. But I strongly suggest that you don't push the envelope like that. The reality is that you'll know why your prospects demeanour when it's time to move on, meaning when you should run more loops and when you say, you know what, let me set up a follow-up call either over the phone or in person or simply end it for good and just lose the person's number because they're not right for the product and you don't want to waste their time or yours. Now, one important point here is that you'll know at some point like when people are feeling pressured because they'll start like having the laughing, you know, in a kind of a defensive laugh and you'll get overtly feeling pressured, right? In fact, as soon as you sense that your prospect is feeling even the slightest bit like that, they're feeling pressured, you immediately want to pull back and say something along the lines of Jim, Jill, John, Jeremy, you know, please don't misconstrue my enthusiasm for pressure. It's just that I know this truly is a proper fit for you. And then from there, you actually have two options. Option one, you can actually use that statement as an opportunity to loop back into the sale yet again and give it one more college try, paying very, very close attention to both your tonality and your body language to make sure that you steer clear of any type of unconscious communication that speaks even close to absolute certainty or bottle of enthusiasm and instead focus on utter sincerity and I feel your pain. By doing that, you can sort of bring the person back down from feeling pressured into a more comforting state and then from there, you can try to loop back into a close one last time. But again, if at any point there, you sense they're feeling even the slightest bit pressured or perturbed, then I want you to immediately transition into option number two, which is to use this as an opportunity to get back into rapport with your prospect so you can now end the encounter on a high note while also setting up the possibility for a call back. In other words, your follow up campaign. So in this case, you'd say something along the lines of, you know, Jim, Jill, John, Jeremy, please don't misconstrue my enthusiasm or pressure. It's just that I know this truly is a perfect fit for you. And now you change your tonality to one of utter sincerity and you add, so why don't we do this? Let me email you the information you were looking for or whatever their last objection was. I'll give you a few days to look it over. You can wrap your arms around everything and then we can speak then. Sound good. And then you shut up and they'll say, yeah, sure, why not? Now here's the deal. There's two more options. From here, you can either A, set it up so that you are the person that's going to engage the call back. Say, I'll give you a call back and you can actually schedule it. You try to do that, right? Or you can let them call you back. So there's two ways to go. You could put yourself in the driver's seat and I'll call you back in a few days. Obviously you'd give yourself the right to do a call back or say, why don't you give me a call back in a few days after you had a chance to look things over? Why would you do the latter? Well, the truth is this. If you find that when you engage in self-scheduled callbacks that you hardly close anyone, well, I don't want you wasting your time doing that. In other words, you got to just play the numbers. Keep track of this stuff. So if you're scheduling callbacks and even not picking up the phone or no one you speak to buys again, well, guess what? I'm going to want you to leave them in the driver's seat. So when they call you back, now you know that you truly have someone who is interested or else you're going to waste an awful lot of time. Make sense? Okay, great. And now just one final point here before we go to a quiz. One thing you're going to find at this part in the sale is that you're going to start getting what I call BS, not bullshit, buy signals. Buy signals are different than objections. Buy signals are questions or inquiries that the process makes. Like, how much did that cost again? Or how long was the shipping take? Or what's safe about that feature? Those are all buy signals. Those are different than objections. Those are showing that the process is actually very, very close to buying. They're giving you the signal. So the big mistake that people make with buy signals is terrible is that it's also, well, how much does that cost? And you'll say, oh, it's $5,000. That's it. You're lost. When you get a buy signal and you say $5,000 or $1,000, whatever it might be, right? What is that? That's stored energy. Remember energy in benefits out? You can never dangle like that. You can't leave a dangling number. Every time you get a buy signal, I want you to have the discipline to use that as its own little loop and take that buy signal. Say, how much? Oh, it's only $5,000. Energy out. And let me tell you exactly what you're going to get for that. Benefit, benefit, benefit. You stack your benefits once more. You go back down line and say, and believe me, Jim, you're going to be very, very impressed. Sound good? You take it to a close and you shut up. Don't just answer a buy signal. You lose control. You take back control by you answer, you loop back, sum up your benefits, and then go into a soft close. And if you do that, you now are really at the spot. Well, when you perfect this, you can close anyone who's closed more. All right? Now, we're still not done because there's one very, very important thing I have to explain about buy signals. The strategy I just gave you has to do with when you get buy signals in the back end of the sale while you're looping. The strategy for handling buy signals when you're in the front end of the sale before you even made your presentation is very different. In other words, what happens very often in things like automobile sales, when you're selling refrigerators, TVs, is people come in with a lot of knowledge of what they want to buy and they might even have the model picked out. They've done some research and this will hit you with questions that won't let you stay on the straight line. What do you do then? Well, when you get these early stage buy signals, how much does it cost? So they just, you know, give me the price. Well, you cannot be evasive. So you have to answer the person as best you can. Typically what you do is give them a range of pricing, say, listen, there's lots of different options here. You know, it could be as low as this is high as this, but we cater things. We customize things. So it directly, so jeez, I specialize in doing that. In fact, let me ask you a question and then you want to loop back to the same spot on the straight line that you left off and do an abbreviated straight line presentation from wherever you are. In other words, if you were gathering intelligence, well, you're going to keep doing that because you've got to maintain control. You don't want to hop it all over. So he gives you a buy signal. You're like, oh, let me finish. No, no, no. You're an expert. You have questions to ask. You have a presentation to make, but obviously the person is at a higher level of certainty. They're very interested. So you want to be much more, you know, you know, have a lack of ready about the situation. Don't like just say, oh, I'm going to just go quickly, make a shorter presentation and then go right into a close in the same way with your energy in benefits out as for the order softly, effectively. That's how you handle buy signals in the front end. You're going to get this all the time. What happens is, is when you become more proficient with the straight line system, with your tonality in your communication that they says, wow, I'm an expert, you'll get a bit less interrupted by sales. You'll still get them. It'll be more than one thing. Give you the space to essentially unpack their needs and their pain points. I don't get hit that much with disruptive biases, meaning whether just like, won't even let me give my presentation. I'll still get those buy signals. And I use them to build more certain little mini loops as I'm moving down the straight line. So it's almost like a modified going off to Pluto strategy when they give you a buy signal in the early part of the sale. Well, on some level, they kind of started going to Pluto, but it was positive. So you want to use that to sort of, you know, build rapport. So you answer them, but answered in a way it doesn't reveal exactly what your price is, whatever it was, but you've enough information to say feel satisfied and you look back and continue moving forward. And if you sense they're in a rush, like it's a buy signal, I want to get going. You give a abbreviated presentation and boom, you go straight down the line. There you go. The next lesson, we're going to deal with what to do with the people who don't buy it out of the follow up campaigns. All right. It's an awesome lesson. So meet me there after you take your quiz. You're looking good. I'll see you soon.
